standard_description: Measure areas by counting unit squares (square cm, square m, square in, square ft, and improvised units).
sources: NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Geometric Measurement K–5, 2012
Battista, M.T. (2007). The development of geometric and spatial thinking. NCTM Handbook.
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 4 Topic A — Measure Area, Teacher Edition, 2013
Outhred, L. & Mitchelmore, M. (2000). Young children's intuitive understanding of rectangular area measurement. Journal for Research in Mathematics Education, 31(2), 144-167.
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.description: When counting unit squares to find area, student counts some squares twice. Most common on irregular shapes or when no systematic order is used.
misconceptions.example: Rectangle covered by 12 squares. Student counts 14 by accidentally counting a row twice.
misconceptions.citation: Outhred & Mitchelmore (2000) p.156 — 'Without systematic counting strategies (row-by-row or column-by-column), young children frequently double-count or skip unit squares; teaching a left-to-right, top-to-bottom scan reduces this.'
misconceptions.remediation: Count row by row, marking each square with a number or color as counted. Or: count rows × columns for rectangles (5 rows × 3 cols = 15 sq).

misconceptions.description: Student misses some unit squares while counting, leading to underestimation. Opposite of double-counting.
misconceptions.example: Rectangle has 18 squares; student counts 16, missing 2 in a back corner or middle.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM GM Progressions K-5 (2012) p.18 — 'Skipping unit squares is the most common counting error after double-counting; both stem from lack of a systematic scan strategy.'
misconceptions.remediation: Systematic count: start at top-left, move right then down. Touch each square. Mark off completed rows.

misconceptions.description: Student counts only the squares along the edge of the shape, ignoring interior squares. Closely related to area-perimeter confusion.
misconceptions.example: 4×4 rectangle (area = 16). Student counts only boundary = 12 squares.
misconceptions.citation: Battista (2007) p.876 — 'Some students count only the squares along the boundary of a rectangle, conflating area with a perimeter-like count; this error indicates lack of understanding of area as interior coverage.'
misconceptions.remediation: Verify: ALL squares inside, not just edges. Shade the entire interior. Count every shaded square, including center.

misconceptions.short_label: Counting correctly but reporting wrong unit
misconceptions.description: Student counts 12 unit squares correctly but writes '12 cm' or '12 squares' instead of '12 sq cm' (with units).
misconceptions.example: Rectangle on cm grid: area = 12 unit squares. Student writes 12 cm or 12 (no unit).
misconceptions.citation: NCTM GM Progressions K-5 (2012) p.18 — 'Counting accurately but reporting without units (or with the wrong unit) is a persistent error in area measurement; explicit unit labeling must be modeled.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always end with units: 12 sq cm or 12 cm². If unit squares from a non-metric grid, use 'square units' generically.

misconceptions.short_label: Counting 2×1 or larger rectangles as 1 'unit square'
misconceptions.description: Student counts visually-similar but non-unit shapes (e.g., 2×1 rectangles) as if they were unit squares.
misconceptions.example: Grid with mixed 1×1 and 2×1 cells. Student counts each cell as 1, regardless of size.
misconceptions.citation: Outhred & Mitchelmore (2000) p.158 — 'Children initially treat any rectilinear cell as a counting unit without attending to size; insisting on uniform unit squares before counting is essential.'
misconceptions.remediation: Verify each cell is a unit square (same size, square shape) before counting. If cells vary, subdivide into smallest common unit.

misconceptions.short_label: Counting grid intersection dots instead of squares
misconceptions.description: On dot-grid paper, student counts dots at grid intersections instead of unit squares between them. Result: typically (n+1) where n is the correct count.
misconceptions.example: 3×2 rectangle (6 unit squares). Student counts dots at intersections (4×3 = 12) and reports 12.
misconceptions.citation: Battista (2007) p.881 — 'On dot-grid paper, children may count dots rather than squares; the distinction between discrete points and unit-square regions must be explicitly taught.'
misconceptions.remediation: Squares are the SPACES between dots, not the dots themselves. Shade each square. Count shaded regions, not dots.

misconceptions.short_label: Counting squares in a scaled drawing as actual size
misconceptions.description: On a scale drawing (e.g., 1 grid square = 5 ft²), student counts grid squares without applying the scale factor.
misconceptions.example: Scale: 1 square = 4 sq ft. Drawing has 6 squares. Student reports area = 6 sq ft instead of 24 sq ft.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.444 — 'When unit squares represent a multiple of the basic unit, students forget to apply the scale; explicit scale-aware counting is needed for later area work.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Identify scale FIRST (1 sq = ? sq units). Count squares. Multiply count × scale.
